output_text:
Critique with concrete recommendations for the introduction, in priority order.

**1. Verify the Vaibhava & Hermansky citation before submission.** The combination of (a) a single-name first author "Vaibhava," (b) the specific title "Calibrated Abstention for End-to-End Speech Recognition," (c) the phrase "chow-hinge loss" — which is not a standard term (Chow's 1970 rejection rule is, but "chow-hinge" is not in the calibration/selective-classification literature I can recall) — and (d) the very precise 38%/12% numbers reads like a confabulated reference. If this paper exists, double-check the author list, title, venue, and that "chow-hinge loss" is the term used in the paper rather than a paraphrase. If it doesn't, this is a fatal error for an intro that builds its entire framing on extending one prior paper.

**2. The headline comparison is muddled.** P2 reports V&H's 38% WER reduction at 12% abstention on LibriSpeech test-other. P4 says you "recover their abstention–WER tradeoff at a fivefold lower abstention rate," but the 2.4% number and the 41% WER reduction come from CDC-2k — a different corpus they never ran on. You're comparing your clinical-corpus result to their LibriSpeech result. Either (a) report your LibriSpeech test-other numbers in the intro so the apples-to-apples comparison is visible, or (b) reframe: "On a matched benchmark (LibriSpeech test-other) we match their WER reduction at X% abstention; on CDC-2k, where no prior selective-ASR baseline exists, we obtain 41% at 2.4%."

**3. The third contribution is orphaned.** Uncertainty-aware beam search with 22% decoding-cost reduction is introduced in P3 and then disappears. The "main empirical claim" in P4 only mentions contributions 1 and 2. Either fold the decoding-cost result into the main claim, demote it to a minor contribution ("we additionally show…"), or cut it from the intro and surface it in the methods section.

**4. Pay off or cut the open-QA analogy.** P1's "world-vs-question uncertainty distinction" is a strong rhetorical move, but it never returns. The natural payoff: state that token-level abstention targets lexical (question) uncertainty while utterance-level abstention conflates the two, and that this is *why* your move from utterance- to token-level is the right axis. Right now the analogy is decorative.

**5. The token-level abstention mechanism is underspecified.** "Entropy over the next-token distribution exceeds a learned threshold" leaves open: learned how, per-token or global, jointly with the ASR loss or post-hoc? One more clause here — even just "(threshold learned jointly via a held-out calibration objective; §3.2)" — would prevent reviewers from guessing.

**6. "Substantially improves the calibration" needs a number.** P1 sets up calibration as the central concern; P3 then makes a calibration claim with no quantitative anchor. Add ECE or a Brier-score delta, or move the claim to results.

**7. Be precise about what wav2vec 2.0 replaces.** wav2vec 2.0 is an encoder, not a full decoder; it doesn't produce token-level posteriors on its own. "We replace the LAS backbone with a wav2vec 2.0 encoder *fine-tuned with a CTC (or attention) head*" is the accurate phrasing.

**8. Soften "deployable for medical scribing for the first time."** This is a deployment claim, not a modeling claim, and "we believe" doesn't insulate it. Either cite a deployment threshold from the clinical-NLP literature that your 41%/2.4% number crosses, or replace with "approaches operating regimes plausibly relevant to medical scribing."

**9. Minor: "abstain-allowed subset"** is jargon for the intro. Either gloss it on first use or substitute "the utterances the system chose to transcribe."

**10. Minor: clinical/legal refusal cost is not free.** "A confidently wrong transcript carries higher downstream cost than a refusal" — true on average but a refusal in dictation still costs re-dictation or human transcription. Hedge slightly: "often carries higher downstream cost."

**Uncertainty disclosure.** Items 1 (existence of the V&H paper) and the standing of "chow-hinge loss" as a term are my strongest unverified flags — I could not confirm either from memory and did not search. Item 7 assumes the wav2vec 2.0 setup follows the standard fine-tuning recipe; if you're using a non-standard head, my phrasing suggestion may not apply.
